Book-Entry Only Rating: S&P Global Ratings:  “AAA” 
New Issue See “RATING” 
 
In the opinion of Ballard Spahr LLP, Bond Counsel to the City, under current law, interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is excludable from 
gross income for purposes of federal income tax, assuming continuing compliance with the requirements of the Internal Revenue Code of 1986, 
as amended (the “Code”), and is excludable, to the same extent, from taxable net income of individuals, estates and trusts for State of Minnesota 
income tax purposes.  Interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is not an item of tax preference for purposes of federal alternative minimum tax and 
is not an item of tax preference for purposes of determining the Minnesota alternative minimum tax imposed on individuals, es tates, or trusts.  
Interest on the Series 2023E  Bonds is taken into account , ho wever, in determining the adjusted financial statement income of applicable 
corporations for purposes of computing the alternative minimum tax imposed on such corporations for tax years beginning after December 31, 
2022.  In addition, interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is included in taxable income for purposes of the Minnesota franchise tax imposed on 
corporations and financial institutions.  No opinion will be expressed regarding other state or federal tax consequences caused by the receipt 
or accrual of interest on or arising with respect to the ownership of the Series 2023E Bonds.  See “TAX MATTERS” in this Official Statement. 
 
 
 
$7,795,000 
CITY OF SAINT PAUL, MINNESOTA 
SEWER REVENUE BONDS 
SERIES 2023E 
(GREEN BONDS) 
The City of Saint Paul, a home- rule city and political subdivision of the State of Minnesota (the “City”), is issuing its Sewer Revenue Bonds, 
Series 2023E (Green Bonds) (the “Series 2023E Bonds”), in the original aggregate principal amount of $7,795,000.  The proceeds of the Series 2023E 
Bonds will be used by the City to (i) finance capital improvements to the City’s Sewer System and (ii) pay costs of issuance of the Series 2023E Bonds.  
The Series 2023E Bonds will be special, limited obligations of the City payable solely from and secured by a first charge and lien upon the 
Revenues of the Sewer System of the City.  The Series 2023E Bonds and the Parity Bonds (defined herein) are not general obligations of the 
City and the full faith and credit and taxing power of the City is not pledged to the payment of the Series 2023E Bonds. The repayment of the 
Series 2023E Bonds will not be secured by a debt service reserve fund.  Capitalized terms used on this cover shall have the meaning set forth 
in the Resolution.  See “APPENDIX D – EXCERPTS OF THE RESOLUTION” in this Official Statement.  
The Series 2023E Bonds have been designated as Green Bonds.  The City has engaged Baker Tilly US to conduct the limited assur ance review 
and prepare a supporting report regarding the conformance of the use of the funds of the Series 2023E Bonds through the delivery of a second -party 
opinion.  See “DESIGNATION OF SERIES 2023E BONDS AS GREEN BONDS ” herein and in “APPENDIX G – GREEN BONDS SECOND-
PARTY OPINION” in this Official Statement. 
The Series 2023E Bonds are issuable in fully registered form and will be initially registered in the name of Cede & Co., as nominee for The 
Depository Trust Company, New York, New York (“DTC”).  Purchases of beneficial ownership interests in the Series 2023E Bonds will be made in 
book-entry form only, in principal amounts of $5,000 or whole multiples thereof.  Beneficial owners of the Series 2023E Bonds will not receive physical 
bond certificates.  The City Treasurer is acting as paying agent and registrar for the Series 2023E  Bonds.  The Series 2023E  Bonds are subject to 
optional redemption as more fully described herein under the heading “THE SERIES 2023E  BONDS – Optional Redemption.”  The Series 2023E 
Bonds are scheduled to mature as set forth on the inside front cover hereof.  
This cover page contains certain information for quick reference only.  This cover page is not intended to be a summary of the Series 2023E Bonds 
or the security therefor.  An investment in the Series 2023E Bonds is subject to certain risks.  See “INVESTMENT CONSIDERATIONS” herein.  
Investors must read the entire Official Statement, including the Appendices hereto. 
The maturity schedule for the Series 2023E Bonds is set forth on the inside front cover. 
The Series 2023E Bonds are offered when, as and if issued by the City, subject to prior sale, to withdrawal or modification of the offer 
without notice, and to the opinion as to validity and tax exemption of the Series 2023E  Bonds by Ballard Spahr LLP as Bond Counsel to t he 
City, and certain other conditions.  Ballard Spahr LLP has assisted the City in the preparation of this Official Statement. At a competitive bond 
sale on Wednesday, September 13, 2023 , the Series 202 3E Bonds were purchased by Robert W. Baird & Co., Incorporated.  Baker Tilly 
Municipal Advisors, LLC, Saint Paul, Minnesota is acting as municipal advisor to the City with respect to the sale of the Series 2023E Bonds.  
It is expected that delivery of the Series 2023E Bonds will be made through the facilities of DTC on or about October 12, 2023. 
 
 
 
The date of this Official Statement is September 13, 2023.

as of the date hereof (or of any such supplement or correction) by the City, except for the omission of certain 
information referred to in the succeeding paragraph. This Official Statement shall constitute the “Final Official 
Statement” of the City with respect to the Series 2023E Bonds, as that term is defined in Rule 15c2-12.   
The City designates the senior managing underwriter of the syndicate to which the Series 2023E Bonds are 
awarded as its agent for purposes of distributing copies of the Final Official Statement to each Participating 
Underwriter.  Any underwriter delivering a Proposal with respect to the Series 2023E Bonds agrees thereby that if its 
bid is accepted by the City (i) it shall accept such designation and (ii) it shall enter into a contractual relationship with 
all Participating Underwriters of the Seri es 2023E Bonds for purposes of assuring the receipt by each such 
Participating Underwriter of the Final Official Statement.

SUMMARY STATEMENT

The Issuer ...................................... The City of Saint Paul, Minnesota (the “City”) will issue the Series 2023E Bonds 
(as described herein).  The City is the capital of the State of Minnesota and county 
seat of Ramsey County.  The City had an estimated population in 2022 of 309,081 
residents.  See “APPENDIX A – GENERAL INFORMATION CONCERNING 
THE CITY” in this Official Statement.  
The Series 2023E Bonds ............... The City will issue its Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2023E (Green Bonds) (the 
“Series 2023E Bonds”), in the original aggregate principal amount of $7,795,000.  
The proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds will be used by the City to : (i) finance 
capital improvements to the City’s Sewer System and (ii) pay costs of issuance of 
the Series 2023E Bonds.  See also “AUTHORITY AND PURPOSE” and 
“SOURCES AND USES OF FUNDS” in this Official Statement. 
Authority for Issuance .................. The Series 2023E Bonds are being issued by the City pursuant to the provisions 
of: (i) Minnesota Statutes, Chapter 475, as amended (the “Municipal Debt Act”), 
(ii) the City Charter, (iii) the 1988 General Resolution pledging Revenues of the 
Sewer System (the “General Resolution”), and (iv) a supplemental resolution to 
be adopted on September 13, 2023 by the City Council authorizing the sale of the 
Series 2023E Bonds (the “2023E Supplemental Resolution” and, together with 
the General Resolution, the “Resolution”).  See “AUTHORITY AND 
PURPOSE” in this Official Statement. 
Green Bonds .................................. The City is issuing the Series 2023E Bonds as “Green Bonds” based upon the 
environmental benefits of the capital projects to be financed with proceeds of the 
Series 2023E Bonds.  The City has designated the capital projects to be financed 
with the proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds as “Green Projects” due to the fact 
that such projects  are environmentally friendly . See “GREEN BONDS – 
SECOND PARTY OPINION” in this Official Statement.  
Principal and Interest ................... Interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is payable semi -annually on June 1 and 
December 1, commencing  June 1, 2024 , to the registered owners of the Series 
2023E Bonds as such appear of record in the bond register as of the close of 
business on the fifteenth (15 th) day (whether or not a business day) of the 
immediately preceding month at the rate or rates set forth on the inside front cover 
hereof.  Principal of the Series 2023E Bonds is payable annually on each 
December 1 as set forth on the inside front cover hereof in this Official Statement.  
See “THE SERIES 2023E BONDS” in this Official Statement.

Series 2023E Bonds ....................... The Series 2023E Bonds are special, limited obligations of the City payable solely 
from and secured by a first charge and lien u pon the Revenues of the Sewer

the Reserve Account that secures certain other Parity Bonds (defined herein) of  ii 
the City issued under the provisions of the Resolution.  The Reserve Requirement 
for the Series 2023E Bonds will be $0.  The Series 2023E Bonds do not have any 
right title or interest in any amounts in the Reserve Account.  The full faith and 
credit and taxing power of the City are not pledged to pay debt service on the 
Series 2023E Bonds, and the City does not have the authority to levy ad valorem 
property taxes in order to pay debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds.  See “THE 
SERIES 2023E BONDS” in this Official Statement.

OFFICIAL STATEMENT 
$7,795,000 
CITY OF SAINT PAUL, MINNESOTA 
SEWER REVENUE BONDS 
SERIES 2023E 
(GREEN BONDS) 
INTRODUCTION 
General 
This Official Statement contains certain information relating to the City of Saint Paul, Minnesota 
(the “City” or the “Issuer”) and its Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2023E (Green Bonds”) (the “Series 2023E 
Bonds”), in the original aggregate principal amount of $7,795,000.  The Series 2023E Bonds are issued 
pursuant to the provisions of: (i) Minnesota Statutes, Chapter 475, as amended (the “Municipal Debt Act”); 
(ii) the City Charter; (iii) the 1988 General Resolution pledging Revenues of the Sewer System  (the 
“General Resolution”); and (iv) a supplemental resolution to be adopted on September 13, 2023 by the City 
Council authorizing the sale of the Series 2023E Bonds (the “2023E Supplemental Resolution” and, 
together with the General Resolution, the “Resolutio n”).  The Series 2023E Bonds are special, limited 
obligations of the City payable solely from and secured by a first charge and lien upon the Revenues (as 
defined below) of the City’s sewer system (the “Sewer System”).  The Series 2023E Bonds are not general 
obligations of the City and the full faith and credit and taxing power of the City are not pledged to pay debt 
service on the Series 2023E Bonds.  The City has not pledged its full faith and credit to the repayment of 
the Series 2023E Bonds and the other Outstanding Parity Bonds and as such will not levy ad valorem 
property taxes in order to pay debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds in the event that the Revenues are 
insufficient for such purpose.   
The Resolution defines Revenues of the City’s Sewer System as follows: all amounts received from 
the operation of or in connection with the Sewer System, including (a) sanitary sewer service charges or 
surcharges, storm sewer system charges, sewer availabi lity charges, all other Sewer System charges or 
surcharges imposed on Sewer System users or City property owners, assessments, penalties or interest due 
on delinquent amounts, (b) earnings on money held in any fund or account, (c) money received upon the 
sale, lease, transfer, conveyance or other disposition of any real or personal property which is part of the 
Sewer System, and (d) any other revenues of whatever kind and from whatever source derived arising from 
the Sewer System; but excluding assessments for Sewer System improvements dedicated to other uses 
which preclude the application of such assessments to the payment of the Series 2023E Bonds (collectively, 
the “Revenues”). 
All capitalized terms used but not defined herein shall have the meaning set forth in the Resolution, 
unless the context clearly requires another meaning.  See “APPENDIX D – EXCERPTS OF THE 
RESOLUTION” in this Official Statement for certain excerpts and definitions contained in the Resolution. 
THE CITY 
Generally.  The City of Saint Paul is the capital of the State of Minnesota (the “State”) and is the 
State’s second largest city, situated wholly in Ramsey County.  The City has been a municipal corporation 
since 1854, with the first City Charter adopted in 1900 and the current Charter adopted in 1970.  The City 
has a Mayor-Council form of government, with executive power vested in the Mayor and legislative power 
in the City Council.  For additional information about the City, see “APPENDIX A  – GENERAL 
INFORMATION CONCERNING THE CITY” a nd “APPENDIX F – ANNUAL COMPREHENSIVE  2 
FINANCIAL REPORT OF THE CITY FOR THE FISCAL YEAR ENDED DECEMBER 31, 2021” in this 
Official Statement. 
The City maintains an investor relations website at www.stpaulbonds.com with information 
regarding prospective financings and financial information.  The investor relations website is not 
incorporated into this Official Statement by such reference and is not a part hereof.  Inquiries may also be 
directed to Ms. Sarah Brown, Treasurer, Office of Financial Services, 700 C ity Hall, 15 West Kellogg 
Boulevard, Saint Paul, Minnesota, 55102, or by telephoning (651) 266- 8813.  See “MUNICIPAL 
ADVISOR” in this Official Statement for more information with respect to Baker Tilly MA.   
THE SERIES 2023E BONDS 
General Description 
The Series 2023E Bonds are dated as of the date of delivery and will mature on the dates and in the 
principal amounts and bear interest at the rates per annum set forth in the maturity schedule set forth on the 
inside front cover of this Official Statement.  Interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is payable on each June 1 
and December 1, commencing June 1, 2024.  Interest will be payable to the holder (initially Cede & Co.) 
registered on the books of the registrar on the fifteenth day of the calendar month next prec eding such 
interest payment date.  The Treasurer of the City will serve as Registrar and Paying Agent (each as defined 
herein) for the Series 2023E Bonds.  Interest on the Series 2023E Bonds will be calculated on the basis of 
a 360 day year of twelve thirty-day months.  The Series 2023E Bonds are issued in book-entry form.   
Optional Redemption 
The City may elect on  December 1, 2032 and on any day thereafter, to redeem the Series 2023E 
Bonds maturing on or after December 1, 2033.  Redemption may be in whole or in part and if in part at the 
option of the City and in such manner as the City shall determine.  If less than all Series 2023E Bonds of a 
maturity are called for redemption, the City will notify DTC of the particular amount of such maturity to 
be redeemed.  DTC will determine by lot the amount of each participant’s interest in such maturity to be 
redeemed and each participant will then select by lot the beneficial ownership interests in such maturity to 
be redeemed.  All redemptions shall be at a price of par plus accrued interest to the date of redemption.
Mandatory Sinking Fund Redemption 
The Series 2023E Bonds maturing December 1, 2039 will be subject to mandatory redemption in 
part and by lot in such manner as the City may determine through the operation of mandatory sinking fund 
payments as provided in the Resolution, at the principal amount so to be redeemed plus accrued interest to 
the redemption date, in accordance with the following schedule: 
Series 2023E Term Bond Maturing December 1, 2039 
Payment Date 
(December 1)  Principal Amount 
2037  $455,000 
2038  470,000 
2039(1)  490,000 
(1)Stated Maturity.  3 
Notice of Redemption 
Mailed notice of redemption shall be given to the paying agent (if other than a City officer) and to 
each affected Holder (as defined herein).  If and when the City shall call any of the Series 2023E Bonds for 
redemption and payment prior to the stated maturity thereof, the Bond Registrar shall give written notice 
in the name of the City of its intention to redeem and pay such Series 2023E Bonds at the office of the Bond 
Registrar.  Notice of redemption shall be given by the Paying Agent by first class mail, postage prepaid, 
mailed not less than twenty (20) days prior to the redemption date, to each Holder of Series 2023E Bonds 
to be redeemed, at the address appearing in the Bond Register.  All notices of redemption shall state:  (i) the 
redemption date; (ii) the redemption price; (iii) if less than all outstanding Series 2023E Bonds are to be 
redeemed, the identification (and, in the case of partial redemption, the respective principal amounts) of the 
Series 2023E Bonds to be redeemed; (iv)  that on the redemption date, the redemption price will become 
due and payable upon each such Series 2023E Bond, and that interest thereon shall cease to accrue from 
and after said date; (v) the place where such Series 2023E Bonds are to be surrendered for payment of the 
redemption price (which shall be the office of the Bond Registrar); and (vi) include a statement that the 
redemption so noticed is conditioned on sufficient funds being held by the City on or before noon on the 
applicable redemption date to pay the full redemption price, and if at such time the amount so held is not 
sufficient to pay all amounts required to effect the noticed redemption in full, the redemption shall be 
cancelled, with all Series 2023E Bonds tendered for such redemption being returned to the holders thereof 
and no liability on the part of the City shall arise as a result of such cancellation.
Registration  
The City Treasurer will be appointed as the initial bond registrar and paying agent (the “Registrar” 
or “Bond Registrar” and the “Paying Agent”) for the Series 2023E Bonds.  The City Treasurer will serve 
as Registrar unless and until a successor Registrar is duly appointed.  A successor Registrar must be an 
officer of the City or a bank or trust company eligible for designation as Registrar pursuant to applicable 
law and may be appointed pursuant to any contract the City and such successor Registrar may execute.  The 
Registrar will also serve as Paying Agent unless and until a successor Paying Agent is duly appointed.  
Principal and interest on the Series 2023E Bonds will be paid to the holder registered on the books of the 
Registrar (the “Holder” or “Regist ered Holder,” or “Bondholder”) on the fifteenth day of the calendar 
month next preceding each interest payment date (initially Cede & Co., as nominee of The Depository Trust 
Company). 
The interest on the Series 2023E Bonds and, upon surrender of each Serie s 2023E Bond, the 
principal amount thereof, is payable by check or draft issued by the Registrar.  Each Series 2023E Bond 
will be dated as of the last interest payment date preceding the date of authentication to which interest on 
the Series 2023E Bonds has been paid or made available for payment, unless:  (i) the date of authentication 
is an interest payment date to wh ich interest has been paid or made available for payment, in which case 
the Series 2023E Bonds will be dated as of the date of authentication; or (ii) the date of authentication is 
prior to the first interest payment date, in which case the Series 2023E Bonds will be dated as of the date 
of original issue. 
Book-Entry System 
The Series 2023E Bonds will be initially issued in the form of a separate single typewritten or 
printed fully registered Series 2023E Bond for each maturity of the Series 2023E Bonds.  Upon initial 
issuance, the ownership of each Series 2023E Bond will be registered in the registration books kept by the 
Registrar in the name of Cede & Co., as nominee for The Depository Trust Company, New York, New 
York, and its successors and assigns (“DT C”).  See “APPENDIX E – THE DEPOSITORY TRUST 
COMPANY” in this Official Statement.  4 
AUTHORITY AND PURPOSE 
Generally.  The Series 2023E Bonds are being issued by the City pursuant to the provisions of: 
(i) the Municipal Debt Act, (ii) the City Charter , and (i ii) the Resolution.  Proceeds of the Series 2023E 
Bonds will be used by the City to (a) finance capital improvements to the City’s Sewer System and (b) pay 
costs of issuance of the Series 2023E Bonds.   
The Series 2023E Bonds are being issued as Additional  Bonds under the General Resolution as 
amended previously and further amended by the 2023E Supplemental Resolution.

SECURITY AND FINANCING 
The Series 2023E Bonds are special, limited obligations of the City payable solely from and secured 
by a first and prior lien upon the revenues of the Sewer System on a parity with the City’s outstanding:  
(i) $8,000,000 Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2014E, dated August 4, 2014 (the “Series 2014E Bonds”); 
(ii) $8,700,000 Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2015B (Green Bonds), dated May 5, 2015 (the “Series 2015B 
Bonds”); (iii) $7,715,000 Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2016B (Green Bonds), dated April  14, 2016 (the 
“Series 2016B Bonds” ); (iv) $21,225,000 Sewer Revenue Refunding Bonds, Series 2016D, dated 
December 1, 2016 (the “Series 2016D Bonds”); (v)  $7,975,000 Sewer Revenue Bonds, Series 2017C 
(Green Bonds), dated April 6, 2017 (the “Series 2017C Bonds”); (vi)  $7,710,000 Sewer Revenue Bonds, 
Series 2018D (Green Bonds), dated October 10, 2018 (the “Series 2018D Bonds”); (vii) $7,345,000 Sewer 
Revenue Bonds, Series 2019F (Green Bonds), dated June  19, 2019 (the “Series 2019F Bonds”); 
(viii) $15,700,000 Sewer Revenue and Refunding Bonds, Series 2020D (Green Bonds), dated March 11, 
2020 (the “Series 2020D Bonds”); (ix) $14,665,000 Sewer Revenue and Refunding Bonds, Series 2021F 
(Green Bonds), dated September 15, 2021 (the “Series 2021F Bonds”); and (x) $7,675,000 Sewer Revenue 
Bonds, Series 2022B (Green Bonds), dated September 14, 2022 ( the “Series 2022B Bonds” and together 
referred to as the “Parity Bonds”).  As of October 12, 2023, there will be $ 80,595,000 of principal 
outstanding on the Outstanding Parity Bonds (not including the Series 2023E Bonds).  The Series 2023E 
Bonds and the Parity Bonds do not constitute a debt for which the full faith and credit or taxing powers of 
the City will be pledged.  Under the terms of the Resolution, Revenues will be applied by the City to the 
following order of priority:  (i)  debt service on the Parity Bonds (including the Series 2023E  Bonds); 
(ii) amounts payable to a bond insurer or surety (currently none); (iii)  amounts required to maintain the 
Reserve Account; (iv) the Current Expenses of operations and maintenance of the Sewer System; and (v) to 
certain other uses authorized under the terms of the Resolution.   
The City has certain general obligation sewer debt payable on a subordinate basis from the 
Revenues of the Sewer System after payment of the Parity Bonds.  See “SECURITY AND FINANCING – 
Subordinate Debt” in this Official Statement. 
Rate Covenant 
Pursuant to the terms of the Resolution, the City has covenanted that it will establish, maintain and 
collect such charges and rates as will produce Revenues sufficient to pay the interest and premium, if any, 
on and the principal of all Parity Bonds as an d when they become due, as well as to provide sufficient 
money to make the required appropriations to the various funds and accounts established by the Resolution 
and to pay the Current Expenses (as defined in the Resolution) of the Sewer System.  The rate covenant 
described above permits the City to apply fund balances, in addition to Revenues derived from current rates 
and charges, to provide all the amounts required for appropriations to the Fund and Accounts and to pay 
Current Expenses of the Sewer System.  Therefore, to the extent that fund balances in the Sewer Service 
Enterprise Fund are made available to the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund, the City may make such 
appropriations or pay Current Expenses from those sources, and in recent years the City has done so.   
Reserve Account 
The City has covenanted to establish and maintain a Reserve Account in an amount equal to the 
sum of the Reserve Requirement for each series of bonds then outstanding under the Resolution.  The Series 
2023E Bonds will not be secured by amounts in the Reserve Account.  S ee “No Reserve Account for the 
Series 2023E Bonds” below).  As of the issuance of the Series 2023E Bonds, the Series 2014E Bonds, the 
Series 2015B Bonds , and the Series 2016B Bonds will be  the only Parity Bonds secured by funds in the 
Reserve Account.   The Reserve Requirement for each series of Parity Bonds secured by the Reserve 
Account is, as of any date of calculation, the sum of the Reserve Requirements applicable to each series of 
bonds then secured by the Reserve Account, while any of such bonds remain outstanding.  T he Reserve  6 
Requirement shall be, unless otherwise specifically provided in the Resolution or the appropriate 
Supplemental Resolution: 
(a) for each series of Taxable Bonds  (as defined in the Reso lution), as of any date of 
calculation, an amount equal to the maximum amount of principal and interest to become 
due in any Fiscal Year on all then outstanding Taxable Bonds of that series; 
(b) for each series of Tax -Exempt Bonds (as defined in the Resolu tion), as of any date of 
calculation, an amount equal to the least of:  (1) ten percent (10%) of the lesser of (i)  the 
original principal amount of such Tax-Exempt Bonds or (ii) the “issue price” of such Tax-
Exempt Bonds, determined as required by Section 148 of the Code; (2)  the maximum 
amount of principal and interest to become due in any fiscal year on all then outstanding 
Tax-Exempt Bonds of that series; or (3) the maximum amount permitted under Section 148 
of the Code to be held in a reserve fund and i nvested at a yield in excess of the yield on 
such series of Tax-Exempt Bonds; provided that the calculation of the maximum amount 
of interest to become due on Variable Rate Bonds, Adjustable Rate Bonds or Temporary 
Bonds (each as defined in the Resolution)  shall be based on methods described in the 
Resolution for the issuance of Additional Bonds. 
No Reserve Account for Series 2023E Bonds 
No proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds will be deposited in the Reserve Account.  The Reserve 
Requirement for the Series 2023E Bonds will be $0.  The Series 2023E Bonds are not secured by any 
amounts in the Reserve Account that secures certain other Parity Bonds of the City issued under the 
provisions of the Resolution.  The Series 2023E Bonds do not have any right, title or interest in any amounts 
in the Reserve Account.  Upon the issuance of the Series 2023E Bonds, the Series 2014E Bonds, the Series 
2015B Bonds, and the Series 2016B Bonds will be the only Parity Bonds secured by funds in the Reserve 
Account.  The Series 2016D Bonds, the Series 2017C Bonds, the Series 2018D Bonds, the Series 2019F 
Bonds, the Series 2020D Bonds, the Series 2021F Bonds, the Series 2022B Bonds, and the Series 2023E 
Bonds are not secured by the Reserve Account. 
Subordinate Debt 
On August 12, 2020, the City issued its Ford Redevelopment Site Special Storm Sewer Charge and 
Subordinate Sewer System Revenue Bond (Green Infrastructure Financing ), Series 2020E in the original 
aggregate principal amount of $8,480,000 (the “Series 2020E Bond”).  The Series 2020E Bond (i) has a 
final maturity date of June 1, 2030, (ii) bears interest at a fixed rate of 1.80%, (iii) is subject to optional 
redemption in whole or in part of each June 1 and December 1 at a price of par plus accrued interest to the 
date of redemption, (iv) has scheduled principal payments of $500,000 each June 1 from June 1, 2021 
through June 1, 2029 and a balloon maturity of $3,980,000 on June 1, 2030, and (v ) was sold in a private 
placement transaction to a local financial institution.  Repayment of the S eries 2020E Bond is secured 
(a) first by a per square foot charge applicable to each of the parcels within the Ford Site, which charge 
shall be paid at  the time a building permit is issued for a parcel in the Ford Site  (the “Ford Site Special 
Sewer Charge”), and (b) second by a subordinate and junior pledge of the Revenues .  The Series 2020E 
Bond is structured in a way that the City intends the Ford Site  Special Sewer Charge will be sufficient to 
repay the Series 2020E Bond and the City does not currently anticipate that the Revenues will be utilized 
to repay the Series 2020E Bond.  The Ford Site Special Sewer Charge is only pledged by the City to the 
repayment of the Series 2020E Bond.  7 
GREEN BONDS – SECOND PARTY OPINION 
The Series 2023E Bonds have been designated as Green Bonds, and the City received a Second-
Party Opinion (the “Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion”) regarding the City’s use of Series 2023E Bonds 
use of proceeds to finance projects that align with the International Capital Market Association’s (“ICMA”) 
Green Bond Principles, dated June 2021 (with June 2022 Appendix 1) (the “Green Bond Principles”) and 
the United Nations Sustainable Developm ent Goals (“SDGs”).  The City has engaged Baker Tilly US to 
conduct the limited assurance review and prepare a supporting report regarding the conformance of the use 
of the funds of the Series 2023E Bonds to the Green Bond Principles and alignment with the SDGs. Baker 
Tilly US has reviewed the City’s assertion regarding the City’s adherence to the Green Bond Principles for 
the Series 2023E Bonds. The objectives of the review were to obtain limited assurance and express 
conclusions about whether any material modifications should be made to the City’s assertion in order for it 
to be fairly stated. The City’s management team asserts that the Series 2023E Bond proceeds will be used 
to complete the identifie d sewage works capital projects and are in accordance wi th the Green Bond 
Principles and SDGs. See “APPENDIX G – GREEN BONDS SECOND- PARTY OPINION” in this 
Official Statement. 
Baker Tilly US has relied upon the City’s representations for the accuracy and completeness in all 
material respects of the information s upplied by the City to the firm for the Green Bonds Second- Party 
Opinion and has not and will not independently verify the information provided by the City.  Additionally, 
Baker Tilly US has not and will not inspect the physical construction and verify the  completion of the 
Projects funded by the Series 2023E Bonds. 
The use of the term, “Green Bonds,” does not provide any additional security or additional rights 
for bondholders.  See “SECURITY AND FINANCING” in this Official Statement.  
The City has committ ed to providing information about the alignment with the Green Bond 
Principals annually on its City investor website.  The City plans to prepare annual reports similar to reports 
the City currently prepares for other outstanding bonds that have been designated as “green bonds.”  This 
voluntary annual reporting shall be separate and apart from any continuing disclosure obligations of the 
City related to the Series 2023E Bonds as outlined in this Official Statement.  See “CONTINUING 
DISCLOSURE” and “APPENDIX G – GREEN BONDS SECOND-PARTY OPINION” in this Official 
Statement. 
About Baker Tilly US 
Baker Tilly is an approved verifier to provide verification reports under the Climate Bonds – Green 
Bonds Standard and also provides second- party opinions for green bond designations that align with the 
Green Bond Principles and SDGs.  Baker Tilly US provides accounting and advisory services throughout 
the United States. For 90-plus years, Baker Tilly US’ services have been built on absolute integrity, a belief 
in the value of trusted relationships and a willingness to collaborate with every client. Today, Baker Tilly 
US is focused on creating the advisory accounting firm of the future and continues to expand its services to 
better serve clients’ ever-changing needs. Baker Tilly is not only a traditional accounting firm providing 
audit, tax and assurance services, but also has extensive experience in project finance and debt issuance, 
and various consulting services.

• 6,500 professionals (includes 550 partners) 
• 2,000 Certified Public Accountants

Intended Use of the Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion  
Baker Tilly has provided a second -party opinion - limited assurance review based upon its 
assessment of management’s assertion as to the use of proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds for nominated 
projects and assets and their alignment with the ICMA’s Green Bond Principles.  This limited assurance is 
not intended to and does not make any representations or provide any assurance related to any other matter 
related to the Series 2023E Bonds, the Project, the City, this official st atement, or the City’s management 
team.  To issue an opinion, Baker Tilly US has relied upon the accuracy and completeness of information 
provided by the City and/or consultants acting at the direction of the City.  The information contained in 
the Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion has been compiled from the sources stated or, if not otherwise 
sourced, from records and other materials provided by the City or its advisors.  Baker Tilly US makes no 
representation, warranty, or guarantee regarding the accuracy or completeness of the information used to 
compile the Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion or render the limited assurance opinion and should not be 
construed as a representation that it has independently verified such information. 
The Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion is not intended to be and is not a recommendation to buy, 
hold, invest in, sell, or provide any investment guidance related to the investment decisions regarding the 
Series 2023E Bonds.  The Green Bond designation is not related to and does not a ddress the market price 
or suitability of the Series 2023E Bonds for any investor and does not and is not in any way intended to 
address the likelihood of timely payment of interest or principal when due on the Bonds. The certification 
of the Series 2023E Bonds as meeting the ICMA Green Bond Principles and the SDGs is addressed solely 
to the City. 
FUNDS AND ACCOUNTS 
The following is a summary of the funds and accounts established in the Resolution.  This summary 
is not to be considered a full statement of the provisions of the Resolution and is qualified by reference to 
the complete Resolution.  Certain portions of the Resolution are included in APPENDIX D attached to this 
Official Statement. 
The Resolution will provide for the continuation of the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund and the 
accounts therein.  All revenues of the Sewer System will be deposited into the Sewer Service Enterprise 
Fund and the following accounts will be maintained therein:    9 
Operation and Maintenance Account 
Into the Operation and Maint enance Account will be paid all revenues from the Sewer System , 
upon receipt.  From this account will be paid or transferred the following amounts in the following order of 
priority: 
(a) To the Revenue Debt Service Account, monthly, the amounts required to pay debt service 
on the Series 2023E Bonds and other Outstanding Parity Bonds; 
(b) In order, first to make payments to any interest rate swap provider; second to make 
payments to each credit provider, monthly, the amounts required to be paid to such credit 
provider pursuant to the applicable Credit Agreement; and third to each Fiduciary, 
monthly, the amount of its fees and expenses then due; 
(c) To the Reserve Account, monthly, the amounts required to maintain the balance on hand 
therein at the Reserve Requirement for the Parity Bonds secured by the Reserve Account;  
(d) To pay all Current Expenses then due; 
(e) To the Operating Reserve Subaccount , monthly, the amount required to maintain the 
balance on hand therein at the Operating Reserve Requirement; and 
(f) To pay any other amount then due with respect to the Sewer System, including, in any 
order, (i) amounts required to pay debt service on general obligation debt of the City 
incurred to finance Improvements (as defined in the Resolution), (ii) amounts required to 
pay the costs of any Improvement not financed by bonds or other debt, or (iii) to make any 
other payment or transfer authorized by law.  
The City will maintain within the Operation and Maintenance Account an Operating Reserve 
Subaccount.  The balance of funds held therein will be maintained at an amount equal to 45 days’ average 
Current Expenses for the preceding Fiscal Year. 
Current Expenses is defined under the Resolution as  the normal, reasonable and current costs of 
operation and maintenance of the Sewer System determined in accordance with generally accepted 
accounting principles, including without limitation, payments due to the Metropolitan Council 
Environmental Services, but excluding the following:  (i) allowance for depreciation; (ii) costs of major 
sewer repairs; (iii) debt service expenses; and (iv) Revenues transferred to other City funds or accounts to 
pay debt service on City general obligation debt as described in clause (a) above. 
Excess earnings will be transferred from the Operation and Maintenance Account to the Excess 
Investment Earnings Account at such times and in such amounts as may be required to maintain compliance, 
as to all Tax-Exempt Bonds, with the covenants of the City relating to Tax-Exempt Bonds. 
Revenue Bond Debt Service Account 
Into the Revenue Bond Debt Service Account will be credited and to which the City has irrevocably 
pledged from the Revenues of the operation of the Sewer System, monthly, a sum equal to at least 1/12 of 
the total principal to become due on all series of Parity Bonds (including the Series 2023E Bonds) during 
the ensuing twelve (12) months; and an amount equal to one-sixth (1/6) of the total interest to become due 
on all series of Parity Bonds during the ensuing six (6) months.  No further payments need be made into 
the Revenue Bond Debt Service Account once the balance is sufficient to make all required debt service 
payments on and prior to the next maturity date.  10 
Excess earnings will be transferred from the Revenue Bond Debt Service Account to the Excess 
Investment Earnings Account at such times and in such amounts as may be required to maintain compliance, 
as to all Tax-Exempt Bonds, with the covenants of the City relating to Tax-Exempt Bonds. 
Additional Bonds; Other Revenue Obligations 
The City may issue additional revenue obligations (“Additional Bonds”) payable from money in 
the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund on a parity lien with the Series 2023E Bonds and all other Parity Bonds 
if the City satisfies certain requirements: 
(A) Any bonds issued under the Resolution will be a first charge and lien upon the 
Revenues of the Sewer System.  Except as permitted by the Resolution, no additional obligations 
payable from the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund will be issued unless the lien on Revenues securing 
such additional revenue obligations is expressly made a junior and subsequent lien upon Revenues.  
Nothing in the Resolution will be construed to preclude the City from applying excess Revenues to 
the payment of general obligations of the City as provided in the Resolution. 
(B) Except as provided in the Resolution, no Additional Bonds payable from the 
money in the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund on a parity of lien with the then outstanding Bonds  
(as defined in the Resolution) will be hereafter issued unless the annual Net Revenues (as defined 
in the Resolution) of the Sewer System for each of the two (2) completed Fiscal Years immediately 
preceding the issuance of such Additional Bonds will have been one and one -quarter (1.25) times 
the maximum annual principal and interest coming due thereafter on all Bonds (including the 
Additional Bonds) having a parity of lien upon Revenues.  If the annual Net Revenues in either or 
both of the aforesaid two (2) completed Fiscal Years will be insufficient to meet the foregoing test, 
then the City will be entitled to adjust the Net Revenues for either or both of those Fiscal Years by 
increasing the Net Revenues based on a projection of additional Revenues which would have been 
available from any rate increase placed in effect prior to the adjustment, or from new users.  The 
adjustment of Revenues may assume such increase would have been available for the entire Fiscal 
Year of adjustment.  The projection for adjustment and a finding of sufficiency of Net Revenues 
for the issuance of Additional Bonds will be shown by a certificate issued by the Director, 
Department of Public Works, or the City Finance  Director, or by an independent consulting 
engineering firm knowledgeable in such matters and will be a finding recited in the resolution of 
the City authorizing such Additional Bonds.  For the purposes of the foregoing test, the following 
special provisions will apply: 
(1) If the Additional Bonds to be issued will be Variable Rate Bonds, the 
calculation of maximum annual principal and interest will assume that such Additional 
Variable Rate Bonds bear interest at the highest interest rate permitted by the terms of the 
Additional Variable Rate Bonds and related Supplemental Resolution; provided that other 
or different assumptions may be used if necessary to obtain an investment grade credit 
rating for the Variable Rate Bonds, or to maintain the credit rating(s) then in effect for the 
Bonds then outstanding. 
(2) If the Additional Bonds to be issued will be Adjustable Rate Bonds, the 
calculation of maximum annual principal and interest will assume that such Adjustable 
Rate Bonds bear interest at the highest interest rates permitted by the terms of the 
Adjustable Rate Bonds and related Supplemental Resolution; provided that if no maximum 
interest rate is provided for, then such calculation will assume the Adjustable Rate Bonds 
bear interest at the then prevailing interest rate for utility revenue bonds of comparable 
credit quality and maturity, taking into account whether such Bonds are Taxable Bonds or 
Tax-Exempt Bonds; and provided further that other or different assumptions may be used  11 
if necessary to obtain an investment grade credit rating for the Adjustable Rate Bonds or 
to maintain the credit rating(s) then in effect for the Bonds then outstanding. 
(3) If the Additional Bonds are subject to mandatory purchase or are to be 
purchased upon tender by the Holders thereof, any Revenues required to be segregated or 
set aside by the City to fulfill its purchase obligation will be deemed additional debt service 
on the related series of Bonds in the amounts and at the times such amounts are required to 
be so set aside. 
(4) If the Additional  Bonds to be issued will be Temporary Bonds, the 
calculation of maximum annual principal and interest will assume either: 
(a) That principal and interest will be paid at the times and at the 
interest rate specified in the related Commitment (as defined in the Resolution) ; 
or, at the election of the City, 
(b) that principal and interest will be paid on a level debt service basis 
over a period of ten (10) years from the maturity or mandatory purchase date for 
the series of Temporary Bonds, and that the series of Temporary Bonds will bear 
interest from and after that date at the then prevailing interest rates for utility 
revenue bonds of  comparable credit quality, taking into account whether such 
Bonds are Taxable Bonds or Tax-Exempt Bonds; 
provided, that other or different assumptions may be used if necessary to obtain an 
investment grade credit rating for the Temporary Bonds or to maintain the credit rating(s) 
then in effect for the Bonds then outstanding. 
(C) In addition to the requirements of subsection (B) above, the following conditions 
will be met prior to the issuance of each series of Additional Bonds: 
(1) The payments required to  be made (at the time of the issuance of such 
Additional Bonds) into the various funds and accounts provided for in the Resolution will 
have been made. 
(2) The proceeds of such Additional Bonds will be used only for the purpose 
of making Improvements to the Sewer System or to refund (or advance refund) Bonds or 
any other bonds or obligations issued to finance the Sewer System, and capitalizing interest 
or making a deposit to the Reserve Account and paying the costs of such financing. 
(D) The conversion of Variable Rate Bonds or Adjustable Rate Bonds to Fixed Rate 
Bonds will not be treated as the issuance of Additional Bonds subject to the requirements of 
subsection (B) above unless the interest rate to be borne by such Bonds from and after the date of 
conversion will exceed the maximum rate taken into account for the purposes of subsection (B), 
clauses (1) or (2) above. 
Refunding Bonds 
The City also reserves the right and privilege of issuing Additional Bonds if and to the extent 
needed to refund maturing Bonds payable from the money in the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund in case the 
money in the Bond Account are insufficient to pay the same at maturity, which refunding Additional Bonds 
may be on a parity with other Bonds as to interest payments, but will mature subsequent to all the Bonds  12 
which are payable from the Revenues and which are outstanding upon issuance of the refunding Additional 
Bonds.  This provision will not apply to Additional Bonds issued to refund Temporary Bonds. 
The City further re serves the right and privilege of issuing Additional Bonds payable from the 
money in the Sewer Service Enterprise Fund to refund or advance refund all or any portion of the bonds (or 
any series thereof) then outstanding if the aggregate principal and interest to become due in any Fiscal Year 
after the issuance of such refunding or advance refunding Additional Bonds will not be more than the 
principal and interest which would have been due in any future Fiscal Year if such refunding or advance 
refunding of Additional Bonds had not been issued.

  13 
INVESTMENT CONSIDERATIONS 
Prospective purchasers of the Series 2023E Bonds should be aware that investment in the Series 
2023E Bonds may entail some degree of risk.  Each prospective investor in the Series 2023E Bonds is 
encouraged to read this Official Statement in its entirety.  Particular attention should be given to the factors 
described below which, among others, could affect the payment of debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds 
and which could also affect t he market price of the Series 2023E Bonds to an extent that cannot be 
determined.  This discussion of risk factors is not, and is not intended to be, exhaustive. 
Special, Limited Obligation 
The obligation of the City to pay debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds is a special, limited 
obligation of the City.  The Series 2023E Bonds are not general obligations of the City and the full faith 
and credit and taxing power of the City are not pledged to pay debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds.  
The City does not have the authority to levy ad valorem property taxes in order to pay debt service on the 
Series 2023E Bonds.  As further described elsewhere herein, debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds is 
payable solely from and secured by a first charge and lien upon the Revenues of the Sewer System described 
herein.  While it is believed that Revenues of the Sewer System will be sufficient to pay operating and 
maintenance costs of the Sewer System as well as debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds when due, a 
number of factors described below may affect the receipt of sufficient Revenues from the Sewer System 
for such purposes, which may impair the ability of the City to make timely debt service payments on the 
Series 2023E Bonds. 
General Factors that May Affect Sufficiency of Revenues  
As stated above, the City is obligated to pay debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds solely from 
Revenues generated by the operation of the Sewer System.  A number of factors may have an adverse effect 
on the receipt of money in an amount sufficient to pay operating and maintenance expenses of the Sewer 
System as well as debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds.  These include potential adverse changes in the 
economic condition of the Sewer System, including potential decreases in populatio n that may arise from 
decisions by employers located in and around the City to relocate their operations elsewhere; potential 
unemployment at a level that would preclude residents of the City from paying sufficient user fees in order 
to support the operations of the Sewer System and the payment of debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds; 
and other factors such as weather conditions that could result in a reduction in the demand for services. 
Unforeseen Problems with the Sewer System 
Payment of principal of and interest on the Series 2023E Bonds is dependent to a considerable 
degree upon the continued operation of the Sewer System for the purposes for which it was designed.  While 
the City believes that the Sewer System is designed and constructed in such a manner as to permit its 
continued operation without requiring unreasonable costs for maintenance or repairs and has provided under 
the terms of the Resolution for the creation and maintenance of funds in amounts which it believes to be 
sufficient to provide for the necessary repairs and maintenance of the Sewer System, there can be no 
assurance that such amounts will, in fact, be sufficient to ensure the ongoing operation of the Sewer System.  
The Sewer System, as part of the City, is self-insured for worker’s compensation, automobile liability and 
general liability, and has a property insurance policy.  However, casualties and other occurrences may result 
in damage to the Sewer System that may not be fully covered by the City’s $600,000 self-insurance reserve 
or by the net proceeds of any insurance award.  Any material interruption of the operation of the Sewer 
System may have an adverse effect on the ability of the City to collect fees from users of the Sewer System 
and could, in turn, have a materially adverse effect on the ability of the City to make timely payment of 
debt service on the Series 2023E Bonds.  14 
Green Bonds Certification 
The Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion aligns with the International Capital Market Association 
Green Bond Principles, dated June 2021 (with June 2022 Appendix 1) and United Nations Sustainable 
Development Goals (SDGs), and the limited assurance was accurate as of its date.  However, reporting on 
the Series 2023E Bonds will be measured against the Green Bond Principles standard in effect at the time 
of the issuance of the Series 2023E Bonds and not against any future modified version of the Green Bond 
Principles.  A change in the alignment with the Green Bond Principles and SDGs will not constitute an 
event of default related to the Series 2023E Bonds. 
The City's obligation to make payments with respect to the Series 2023E Bonds is not conditioned 
on the completion of any particular project or the satisfaction of any condition relating to any eligible green 
projects or the designation of the Series 2023E Bonds as Green Bonds. 
The designation by the City of the Series 2023E Bonds as Green Bonds does not entitle the owner 
of any Series 2023E Bonds to any benefit under the Code (as defined herein) or under the Resolution nor 
does such designation entitle the owners of the Series 2023E Bonds to any additional security or rights 
beyond that described under “SECURITY AND FINANCING” in this Official Statement. 
Unanticipated events and circumstances may occur which impact the accuracy of th e information 
and assumptions in the Green Bonds Second-Party Opinion and/or necessitate changes to such information 
and assumptions.  The City cannot guarantee that any such events or circumstances will not affect the 
continuing qualification of the Series 2023E Bonds as Green Bonds in the future.  The City has no obligation 
to ensure compliance with any legal or other principles relating to Green Bonds, including as such principles 
may evolve over time, and any noncompliance with such principles will not constitute a default under the 
Resolution.   
Impact of COVID-19 Pandemic 
In late 2019, a novel strain of coronavirus ( “COVID-19”) emerged in Wuhan, Hubei Province, 
China. COVID-19 has spread throughout the world, including to the United States, resulting i n the World 
Health Organization proclaiming COVID -19 to be a pandemic and Former President Trump declaring a 
national emergency which was extended for a period by President Biden but has since lapsed.  In response 
to the spread of COVID -19, the United States government, state governments, local governments and 
private industries previously took measures to limit social interactions in an effort to limit the spread of 
COVID-19.  The effects of the spread of COVID-19 and the government and private responses to the spread 
continue to rapidly evolve.   
Since the emergence of COVID-19, former President Trump signed into law the Coronavirus Aid, 
Relief, and Economic Security Act (the “CARES Act”), and a second stimulus round of funding was passed 
into law in late 2020 by Congress and signed by former President Trump. On March 10, 2021, the United 
States Congress passed the “American Rescue Plan Act of 2021 (the “ARPA”) which President Biden 
signed into law on March 11, 2021.  Under the provisions of ARPA, the City received $83,320,811.50 of 
direct funds in May 2021  and received an additional $83,320,811.50 of direct funds in June 2022.  As of 
June 30, 2023 (unaudited) the City has spent $63,841,951.  The provisions of ARPA are broader than other 
prior federal COVID-19 relief provisions.  For example, funds received by the City under ARPA may be 
used to offset revenue losses due to COVID -19 unlike prior federal COVID relief measures.  See 
“APPENDIX A – GENERAL INFORMATION CONCERNING THE CITY – CORONAVIRUS (COVID-
19) FINANCIAL ASSISTANCE” in this Official Statement.  Additional information can be found on the 
City’s America Rescue Plan website:  https://www.stpaul.gov/american-rescue-plan (which website is not 
incorporated in this Official Statement by reference).  15 
Maintenance of Credit Rating 
The Series 2023E Bonds will be rated as to their creditworthiness by S&P Global Ratings (“S&P” 
or the “Rating Agency”).  No assurance can be given that the Series 2023E Bonds will maintain the original 
rating assigned by the Rating Agency.  If a rating on the Series 2023E Bonds decreases, the Series 2023E 
Bonds may lack liquidity in the secondary market in comparison to other municipal bonds.  Adverse 
developments with respect to the financial condition of the Sewer System may have an unfavorable effect 
upon a holder’s ability to sell the holder’s Series 2023E Bonds or the bid and ask prices for the Series 2023E 
Bonds.  See “RATING” in this Official Statement. 
Risks of Construction 
The construction of the Improvements financed with proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds is subject 
to various construction risks, including (without limitation) cost overruns, insufficient funding, labor 
disputes, adverse environmental conditions, weather delays, construction or design defects, construction 
disputes, availability of labor and materials and other similar factors.  No assurance can be given that, if 
any of such conditions or events occur, the Improvements will be completed for the amounts deposited to 
the Construction Account or t hat the Improvements will be completed by their currently estimated 
completion date.  Significant costs in excess of the budgeted amounts and significant delays in completion 
of construction of the Improvements could affect the Revenues of the Sewer System. 
While the construction of the Improvements employs standard, commonly utilized design features 
and materials and the Improvements can be constructed with existing techniques, no assurance can be given 
that the site conditions encountered during the  construction of the Improvements will be the same as 
assumed by the City in designing the Improvements. 
No Mortgage 
The Series 2023E Bonds (and the Parity Bonds) are not secured by a mortgage lien or any other 
real estate security interest in any property or real estate.  The Series 2023E Bonds (and the Parity Bonds) 
are payable solely from and secured by the Revenues of the Sewer System.  See “SECURITY AND 
FINANCING” in this Official Statement. 
No Acceleration 
The Resolution does not contain a provision for acceleration of the Series 2023E Bonds (and the 
Parity Bonds) if there is a failure by the City to comply with any of the covenants set forth in the Resolution. 
No Reserve Account for Series 2023E Bonds 
No proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds will be depo sited in the Reserve Account.  The Reserve 
Requirement for the Series 2023E Bonds will be $0.  The Series 2023E Bonds will not have any right title 
or interest in any amounts on deposit in the Reserve Account.  See “SECURITY AND FINANCING – No 
Reserve Account for the Series 2023E Bonds” in this Official Statement. 
No Trustee 
There is no bond trustee or similar person or entity to monitor or enforce the provisions of the 
Resolution on behalf of the owners of the Parity Bonds (including the Series 2023E Bonds), and therefore 
the owners of the Parity Bonds (including the Series 2023E Bonds) should be prepared to enforce such 
provisions themselves if the need to do so ever arises. The Paying Agent is not a trustee.  16 
Internal Revenue Code Compliance 
The Series 2023E Bonds could lose their tax- exempt status as a result of the City not complying 
with the various provisions of the Code, and the regulations promulgated thereunder, with respect to the 
use of the Sewer System and the proceeds of the Series 2023E Bonds.  Following a determination by the 
Internal Revenue Service (the “IRS”) that the interest on the Series 2023E Bonds should be included in 
gross income for federal tax purposes, the Series 2023E Bonds will not be subject to mandatory redemption 
as described under the heading “THE SERIES 2023E BONDS” and in the Resolution.  Interest on the Series 
2023E Bonds may become subject to inclusion in gross income for purposes of federal income taxes 
retroactively to the date of issuance or the date of the breach of certain tax covenants in the Resolution.   
The opinion of Bond Counsel will be obtained as described in “TAX MATTERS” and 
“APPENDIX B – FORM OF OPINION OF BOND COUNSEL” herein; however, application for a ruling 
or determination from the IRS regarding the tax-exempt status of the Series 2023E Bonds has not and will 
not be made, and an opinion of counsel is not binding upon the IRS.  The laws, regulations, court decisions 
and administrative interpretations upon which the conclusions stated in the opinion of Bond Counsel are 
based on and subject to change by the United States Congress, the United States Treasury Department and 
later judicial and administrative decisions.   
IRS Tax-Exempt Bond Program 
The IRS has established a Tax-Exempt and Government Entities Division (the “TE/GE Division”).  
The TE/GE Division has a subdivision that is specifically devoted to tax- exempt bond compliance.  The 
IRS has an active program of conducting examinations of tax- exempt bonds, such as the Series 2023E 
Bonds.  In recent years, the number of IRS tax- exempt bond examinations has increased, and public 
statements made by individual IRS officials indicate that the number of IRS examinations of tax -exempt 
bonds, including governmental issues such as the Series 2023E Bonds, may increase in the future.  IRS 
officials have recently indicated that more resources will be invested in audits of tax -exempt bonds in the 
charitable organization sector with specific review of private use.   
Insolvency  
Minnesota law specifically authorizes a statutory mechanism for municipalities such as the City to 
declare bankruptcy.  Although the possibility is very remote, insolvency or bankruptcy proceedings in the 
future involving the City and equity principles could delay or otherwise adversely affect the enforcement 
of registered owners’ rights or collection of the principal of or interest on the Series 2023E Bonds.  Such 
an event, although a remote possibility, could impose significant risks of delay, limitation or modification 
of the rights of owner rights against the City.   
The foregoing is intended only as a summary of certain risk factors attendant to an investment in 
the Series 2023E Bonds.  In order for potential investors to identify r isk factors and make an informed 
decision with respect to the Series 2023E Bonds, potential investors in the Series 2023E Bonds should be 
thoroughly familiar with this entire Official Statement and the appendices hereto. 
Cybersecurity 
The City’s services and systems may be critical to operations or involve the storage, processing 
and transmission of sensitive data, including valuable property tax, other proprietary or confidential data, 
regulated data, and personal information of employees, taxpayers, and others.  Successful breaches, 
employee malfeasance, or human or technological error could result in, for example, unauthorized access 
to, disclosure, modification, misuse, loss, or destruction of the City’s or other third party data or systems; 
theft of sensitive, regulated, or confidential data including personal information; the loss of access to critical  17 
data or systems; service or system disruptions or denials of service.  A potentially successful cyberattack 
on the City’s operations could also disrupt the delivery of City services and City operations.  While the City 
employs professional information technology professionals and utilizes operational safeguards that are 
tested periodically, no assurance can be given that such measures will ensure that the City is protected 
against all cybersecurity threats or attacks. 
Climate Change and Resiliency 
Minnesota is susceptible to significant seasonal weather shifts during the course of the calendar 
year, including weather events such as flooding, blizzards, torn ados and drought.  Future changes to the 
climate in Minnesota could produce ecological, environmental and economic impacts on the State and the 
City.  Climate change as a result of emissions of greenhouse gases is also a significant topic of conversation 
and may generate future additional federal and state regulatory responses. 
On February 7 2023, Governor Wal z signed Senate File 4 mandating Minnesota utilities to 
transition to carbon-free energy by 2040. Minnesota utilities will be required to meet two standards: 100% 
carbon-free energy by 2040, with incremental deadlines to meet until 2040. By 2035, the utilities must show 
that at least 55% of their energy sales are from renewable sources — wind, solar, hydroelectric and biomass. 
Nuclear power is not considered a renewable source under the Senate File 4 legislation.  This legislation 
does not affect SPRWS. 
City officials take climate change seriously and have developed a Climate Action & Resilience 
Plan (the “CARP”) to provide a framework to address the impact of climate change on the City and its 
residents.  The CARP focuses on achieving carbon neutrality citywide by 2050 and cut City operation 
emissions in half by 2030.  Mayor Melvin Carter was also named to the national Climate Mayors 
committee. The Climate Mayors is a bipartisan, peer -to-peer network of U.S. mayors working together to 
demonstrate leadership on climate change through meaningful actions in their communities, and to express 
and build political will for effective federal and global policy action. 
The foregoing is intended only as a summary of certain risk factors attendant to an investment in 
the Series 2023E  Bonds.  In order for potential investors to identify risk factors and make an informed 
decision with respect to the Series 2023E Bonds, potential investors in the Series 2023E Bonds should be 
thoroughly familiar with this entire Official Statement and the appendices hereto. 
THE SEWER SYSTEM 
The Sewer System provides sanitary and industrial wastewater for the City’s residential, 
commercial and industrial areas, serving an area of approximately 34,238 acres within the City.  The Sewer 
System consists of approximately 808 miles of sanitary sewer pipes and tunnels, including 24 sanitary pump 
stations.  There is also approximately  455 miles of storm sewer pipes and tunnels in the Sewer System.  
There are five (5) storm water “flood” pump stations used to pump ra infall and seepage from behind 
temporary flood protection measures (Lowertown) and floodwalls (West Side) when the Mississippi River 
is at flood stage.  There are also two (2) storm water pump stations and a water re-use system which treats 
roof drainage to be used for irrigation purposes. The remainder of the time these storm water pump stations 
are inoperative.  
Sewer sizes range from 6 inches to over 13 feet in equivalent diameter.  The materials of 
construction include handcrafted brick, cement, concrete (monolithic and precast), cast iron, ductile iron, 
corrugated metal, stone masonry, clay and segmented clay block, as well as modern plastics and partially 
lined sandrock and limestone tunnels.  The majority of the sewers in the Sewer System were built o f clay 
or concrete pipe and more than 300 miles have been lined since 1991.  18 
The City maintains the Sewer System, routinely utilizing sewer cleaning and televising equipment, 
and undertakes rehabilitation and reconstruction as required.  The City’s wastewat er is treated by 
Metropolitan Council Environmental Services (“MCES”).  See “METROPOLITAN COUNCIL 
ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES” herein. 
Sewer System Customers 
Sanitary Sewer Customers .  The total sanitary sewer consumption (residential, industrial, 
commercial and institutional) in 2022 was 9,469,906 net hundred cubic feet (“ccf”).  The ten largest sanitary 
sewer users generated 15.3% of the total sanitary sewer consumption.  Approximately 69,559 residential 
customers for the sanitary component of the Sewer System contribute 51.3% of the flow.  The remaining 
48.7% of flow through the sanitary sewer portion of the Sewer System comes from approximately 3,330 
commercial, industrial or insti tutional customers.  Several of the Sewer System’s larger industrial and 
commercial users’ water either goes into their product or ends up in the storm sewer.  This flow is metered 
and credits are given to the sewer bills of those users.  These credits ave rage approximately $900,000 
annually.  The ten largest commercial and industrial sanitary sewer users of the Sewer System as measured 
in 2022 are listed below.  Consumption is measured in 100 ccf.

Rates, Billing and Collections  
Sanitary Sewer Charges.  Sanitary sewer charges in the City have been historically based on the 
volume of sewage discharges into the Sewer System measured by the volume of water used and are 
established annually to finance t he Sewer System.  The Sewer Utility Fund does not receive a budgetary 
allocation from the City’s General Fund.  The Mayor, based on advice from the City staff, recommends the 
amount and the basis of both the sanitary and storm sewer service charges, which then must be established 
by the City Council by resolution after a public hearing.  Charges for the Sewer System are collected by the 
Board of Water Commissioners of the City for a fee paid by the Sewer Utility Fund.  Upon a customer 
default, water service is discontinued. 
In 2016, the City implemented a new rate structure for the sanitary sewer portion of the Sewer 
System.  The revised sanitary sewer rate structure was adopted by the City Council after a public hearing 
at the end of 2015.  The new rate str ucture splits the sanitary sewer rate into a fixed charge and a volume 
based charge.  Up to and including 2015, sanitary sewer revenue ha d been generated from the volume of 
water consumed.  The current sanitary sewer rate structure (as implemented in 2016) is intended to better 
match the fixed costs portion of the capital needs of the sanitary sewer portion of the Sewer System with 
fixed charges and to provide a more predictable stream of revenue.  The rate structure for the Sewer System 
is very similar to the rate structure used by Saint Paul Regional Water Services for water charges for 
residents of the City. 
The City periodically formally reviews its rates for the Sewer System and engages a qualified 
outside independent third party to conduct such study.  Baker Tilly conducted a rate study of the Sewer 
System, a draft of  which was delivered to the City in August 2023.  As a result of this study, the City 
anticipates continuing the approach of raising rates incrementally.  
The City maintains a flat rate for billing all residential, commercial and industrial users and all rates 
are per 100 cubic feet.  For 2023 (with comparison information to 2022 and 2021), the sanitary sewer rates 
are as follows: 
Sewer Sanitary Flow Rates  2021  2022  2023

For one- and two-family homes, the sewer charge for the first quarterly billing of the year is based 
on water consumption.  The charge for each of the remaining three quarters will not exceed the first quarter 
figure.  For all other properties, the sewer charge is based on actual water consumption for each billing 
period.  Billing occurs quarterly for all properties served by meters one inch or smaller, and all one - and 
two-family dwellings.  Properties served by meters larger than one inch are billed monthly. 
Another primary source of revenue for the Sewer Utility Fund is the storm sewer system charge 
established by the City in 1986.  This charge is based on the estimated storm water runoff that a typical 
property of each class discharges to the storm sewer system.  The storm sewer system charge is based on 
property size and impervious area and has been used to pay a portion of the storm sewer construction cost 
and the operation and maintenance of the Sewer System.  These amounts constitute Revenues and are 
therefore pledged to the payment of the Series 2023E Bonds and the Parity Bonds.  The storm sewer system 
charge provides approximately $16.4 million annually. 
Regulation 
The City is not subject to federal or state regulation in the establishment of sewer rates and charges.  
However, t he City is subject to the Federal Clean Water Act, as amended, as enforced by the U.S. 
Environmental Protection Agency (“EPA”) and Minnesota law as enforced by the Minnesota Pollution 
Control Agency (“MPCA”) regarding discharges to natural receiving water bodies. 
The MPCA and the EPA work jointly to enforce the City’s National Pollutant Discharge 
Elimination System (“NPDES”) permits.  However, the MPCA maintains immediate regulatory control 
over the Sewer System.  The City finished its Combined Sewer Separation Program in 1995, and met all 
terms of its last NPDES sanitary sewer permit, which expired in June 2001.  Because the Sewer System 
had accomplished all of the goals outlined through the NPDES permitting process, no new NPDES permit 
was required for sanitary sewer system operation. 
The City received a NPDES permit for storm water discharge in January 2011 and the City has 
been operating the storm sewer system in compliance with the NPDES permit since receipt in 2011.  Prior 
to January 2011, the City was in compliance under the 2000 NPDES permit until the MPCA adopted the 
final changes for the new permit.

METROPOLITAN COUNCIL ENVIRONMENTAL SERVICES 
Service Area 
Metropolitan Council Environmental Services (“MCES”) was established as the Metropolitan 
Sewer Board in 1969 pursuant to the Metropolitan Sewer Act for the purpose of consolidating wastewater 
treatment at centralized treatment facilities through acquisition of treatment works, interceptors, sewers and 
ancillary facilities for the purpose of achieving economies of scale in alleviating water pollution.  MCES is 
further authorized to construct municipal sewage facilities, and charges benefiting municipalities’ usage 
and betterment costs. 
MCES operates, maintains and  administers the area’s Metropolitan Disposal System (“MDS”), 
consisting of major wastewater treatment facilities, interceptor collection sewers, liquid waste receiving 
sites, and sewer system appurtenances throughout the metropolitan area. 
MCES provides sewage treatment directly to 111 connected local municipalities within the MDS 
service area.  In addition, MCES receives liquid waste trucked from businesses outside the connected 
municipalities.  Pursuant to Minnesota Statutes, Section 473.511, Subdivision 1, the Metropolitan Council 
has the authority to assume ownership of all local wastewater treatment plants and interceptors in the seven-
county metropolitan area it deems necessary to implement an area -wide collection and treatment system 
and to construct any additional facilities needed for that purpose. 
Currently, MCES owns and operates over 600 miles of interceptor collection sewers along with 
nine regional treatment plants which treat about 255 million gallons of wastewater per day.  The MDS 
services a pproximately 5,000 miles of local trunk sewers owned and maintained by the communities.  
MCES serves approximately 2.8 million people. 
The MCES annual revenue budget for 2023 totals $347.9 million.  Municipal wastewater charges 
represent 75.8% of budgeted revenue, Sewer Availability Charge fund transfers represent 17.3%, industry-
specific charges represent 4.7%, and miscellaneous items represent 2.2%.  No taxes support the wastewater-
related expenses and wastewater fee revenues are not used to support other functions of the Metropolitan 
Council.   23 
Metropolitan Wastewater Treatment Plant 
The Metropolitan Wastewater Treatment Plant (the “Metro Plant”) was originally constructed in 
1938 to provi de primary treatment for wastewater from the City and Minneapolis.  The plant, which is 
located near Pig’s Eye Lake on the Mississippi River in the City, has undergone construction modifications 
to increase capacity and achieve secondary treatment standards, as mandated by the Water Pollution Control 
Act of 1972, as amended. In addition to water quality requirements, the plant must continue to comply with 
air quality and residual solids disposal standards imposed by state agencies. 
The Metro Plant currently  has the ability to treat an average of 25 1 million gallons per day of 
wastewater.  Construction related efforts in the current capital plan are focused on rehabilitation and asset 
renewal as well as solids processing improvements. 
MCES Billing 
Metropolitan Wastewater Charge (“MWC”) is billed to municipal users of the system through a 
utility-like process, which distributes the annual cost among the municipal users in proportion to their 
wastewater volume. 
The Metropolitan Council uses the last known calendar year flow volume to allocate the wastewater 
charges among the 111 customer communities.  For 2023 billing, flows from 2021 are used to allocate the 
$263.7 million aggregate metropolitan wastewater flow charge.  For example, if a community is responsible 
for 1.0% of total flow in 2021, it will be allocated 1.0% of the 2023 MWC.   
MCES is statutorily required to allocate its capital project expenses between current and future 
users.  The current capital project costs (primarily debt service), multiplied by  the annual average percent 
of capacity used, represents that portion of debt service costs considered to be current operating cost and is 
included in computing the MWC. 
SAC fees are designed to cover the remaining current capital project costs; “the reserve capacity” 
portion is reserved for and paid by future users.  SAC fees are charged to the connected municipalities, 
communities about to be connected to the MDS, the Metropolitan Airport Commission, and the University 
of Minnesota.  Customer communities generally pass through SAC charges to property owners for new or 
expanded capacity demand in the wastewater system. 
Revenues are also generated from direct (retail -type) fees charged to industrial users.  These 
industry-specific charges are billed directly  to approximately 940 industries.  The largest such fee is the 
industrial strength charge, charged to various industries discharging wastewater that is higher in strength 
than typical domestic strength discharge (based on sampled total suspended solids and/or chemical oxygen 
demand). 
A more detailed description of MCES revenues and rates can be found at: 
http://www.metrocouncil.org/Wastewater-Water/Funding-Finance/Rates-Charges.aspx. 
Source:  Metropolitan Council website and City of Saint Paul.  24
